one.en: COSTS – Where the Applicant seeks that the Respondent pay the costs of enforcement proceedings and the costs application – Where the Respondent oppose…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the Applicant seeks that the Respondent pay the costs of enforcement proceedings and the costs application – Where the Respondent opposes the making of any costs order – Where there is a significant litigation history between the parties including two final hearings and two enforcement proceedings – Where s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) considered – Where the Applicant is the primary carer of the parties’ four children but is in a comfortable financial position – Where the Respondent submits he is under significant financial pressure but his circumstances have been within his control – Where the Respondent’s lack of disclosure during the enforcement proceedings caused the Applicant to incur costs – Where other aspects of the Respondent’s conduct caused the Applicant to incur unnecessary legal costs – Where neither party was wholly unsuccessful – Where certain orders were made by consent during the enforcement proceedings – Where there is no evidence of attempts to settle the costs application – Where order made that the Respondent pay the Applicant’s costs in respect of the enforcement proceedings in a fixed sum which reflects the relevant scales – Where order made that the Respondent pay the Applicant’s costs of the costs application in a fixed sum which is more than the scales but less than on an indemnity basis.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These Reasons deal with an Application in a Proceeding filed by Ms Pinney in which she seeks that Mr Pinney pay her costs in respect of enforcement proceedings brought by her and heard by me on 1 December 2025. She also seeks her costs of the costs application. Mr Pinney opposes the making of any costs order.
The enforcement proceedings involved the enforcement of final child support departure orders which were made on 19 May 2023 after a contested final hearing, as well as a costs order made by a Senior Judicial Registrar on 20 August 2024 in respect of a prior enforcement application brought by Ms Pinney.
During the hearing of the enforcement proceedings, and following judicial indications, Mr Pinney consented to orders which have the effect of making him liable for sums totalling over $100,000. I then only had to determine:
What amount was owed by Mr Pinney in respect of the children’s extra-curricular activities; and
Whether I should make an order restraining Mr Pinney from travelling overseas until he had met his financial obligations.
On 19 December 2025, I handed down judgment on those two issues. I ordered that Mr Pinney owes Ms Pinney $2,016.69 in respect of extra-curricular activities (being an amount which was less than what she had sought), and declined to make an order restraining Mr Pinney from travelling overseas.
In respect of this application, the issues I need to determine are:
Whether there are any circumstances in this matter which make it just to order that Mr Pinney pay Ms Pinney’s costs in respect of the enforcement proceedings;
If so, on what basis should the costs of the enforcement proceedings be paid;
Whether Mr Pinney should be ordered to pay the costs of this costs application and, if so, on what basis those costs should be paid.
For the reasons set out below, I make orders that Mr Pinney pay Ms Pinney’s costs in the amount of $29,665.59, which encompasses both costs in respect of the enforcement proceedings and this costs application.

MATERIAL CONSIDERED

I received into evidence and considered:
An affidavit of Ms Pinney filed 15 January 2026; and
An affidavit of Mr Pinney filed 13 February 2026.
Ms Pinney filed written submissions on 27 February 2026. Mr Pinney filed written submissions on 13 March 2026. I have considered those submissions.

ARE THERE CIRCUMSTANCES IN THIS MATTER WHICH MAKE IT JUST TO MAKE A COSTS ORDER?

Section 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) governs the making of costs orders. The usual rule is that each party bears their own costs. If the Court is satisfied that there are circumstances that justify it doing so, it may make such order for costs as it considers just. In Penfold & Penfold (1980) 144 CLR 311, when considering the equivalent provision in a previous iteration of the Act, the High Court considered that, while there must be justifying circumstances for a costs order to be made, no “additional or special onus” is placed upon applicants in their pursuit of costs. Once justifying circumstances are found, the Court has discretion to award costs.
When deciding whether to make a costs order and, if so, what costs order is just, I am required to have regard to the factors in s 114UB(3):
(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure...;

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
...

The parties’ financial circumstances

At the final hearing in 2023, the parties had available for division between them a property pool valued at approximately $10 million. Orders were made such that Ms Pinney received 62 per cent of the value of that pool. I accept her evidence that she received approximately $5.6 million after the sale of the former matrimonial home and purchased a home for herself and the children for $4.25 million. She repaid various loans and now has access to $200,000 in bank accounts. She has a modest income from part-time work. Mr Pinney is in arrears of child support and has continued not to pay private school fees for the two children who were respectively attending and intended to attend private school, meaning that some of the amounts owed, which were the subject of my orders, have not been paid. Ms Pinney is the primary carer of four school-aged children. Nonetheless, plainly she is in a comfortable financial position.
In the enforcement proceedings, Mr Pinney deposed that he had $2.4 million in July 2024, but his Financial Statement indicated that he had disposed of those funds by June 2025. He deposed that he had no property other than some superannuation, and that he also had debts. He also deposed in the enforcement proceedings that he had resigned from his remunerative employment. He deposes in these proceedings that he has no regular income. His disclosure and evidence at the enforcement hearing were inadequate. I found it was likely that he had “squandered” the significant funds which he had available to him after the implementation of the property orders.
Mr Pinney submitted that the evidence is that he is under “significant financial pressure and risk of insolvency arising from his obligations”. On the assumption this is true, that is in circumstances which were within his control.
In any event, the case law is clear that impecuniosity is no bar to the making of a costs order.

The conduct of the parties

A party’s conduct during the proceedings in respect of which a costs order is sought is relevant not because costs orders play a punitive role (they do not), but because one party’s conduct may inappropriately increase the costs incurred by the other party, such as to make it just that the other party be compensated.
In the enforcement proceedings, Mr Pinney failed to comply with his disclosure obligations to the Court and to Ms Pinney. In my Reasons for Judgment, I concluded that “[t]he reasons for Mr Pinney now being in this financial position are completely opaque”. I am satisfied that the failure to comply with his disclosure obligations unnecessarily increased Ms Pinney’s costs of the enforcement proceedings – including by resulting in the issuing of nine subpoenas on her behalf.
There were other deficiencies in Mr Pinney’s conduct during the proceedings – including a failure to serve an affidavit filed on 23 October 2025, a failure to file an updated Financial Statement, and the withdrawal of an admission, which he had made in his affidavit filed for the purpose of the enforcement proceedings and under cross examination, in respect of his liability for private school fees. As a result, Ms Pinney incurred unnecessary legal costs.

Whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to comply with orders of the Court

This is a significant factor in this case. The enforcement proceedings were necessary because Mr Pinney had failed, over a significant period, to comply with orders of the Court made in 2023. An aspect of the enforcement proceedings before me related to a costs order made in August 2024 in respect of previous enforcement proceedings.
On his own admission, in July 2024 Mr Pinney had approximately $2.4 million. There is no reasonable excuse for his failure to comply with orders of the Court.

Whether either party was wholly unsuccessful

Neither party was wholly unsuccessful. Notwithstanding that par (e) is only concerned with whether or not a party was wholly unsuccessful, it is appropriate to have regard to the fact that one party was unsuccessful, at least in respect of a major aspect of their case, either under par (c) (“the conduct of the parties”) or par (g) (“such other matters as the court considers relevant”). It is important, however, if that is done to also have regard to issues where that party has been successful. Provided I am satisfied that there are justifying circumstances, I may make an order notwithstanding how successful a party has been (s 114UB(7)).
Mr Pinney submits that I should not make a costs order because:
He consented to most of the orders which I made, meaning that “[t]he enforcement proceedings did not culminate in a contested determination of [Ms [Pinney’s]] claims in the manner suggested by [Ms [Pinney’s]] submissions”;
Ms Pinney was not successful in obtaining all that she sought in relation to extra-curricular activities;
As the enforcement application was otherwise dismissed, Ms Pinney was not successful in obtaining the injunction against him in relation to overseas travel.
It is true that the bulk of the matters dealt with in the enforcement application were ultimately resolved by way of consent orders. Nonetheless, a judicial hearing was the context within which those consent orders were made. In oral evidence before a Senior Judicial Registrar on 30 September 2025 and in his affidavit filed 23 October 2025, Mr Pinney had accepted that he owed the sums sought by Ms Pinney, save for those in respect of extra-curricular expenses. However, by affidavit filed 28 November 2025 (which was not admitted into evidence), he resiled from that position and instead claimed he was not liable to pay school fees. It was only once the hearing of the enforcement proceedings had commenced and under the scrutiny of the Court that Mr Pinney consented to the making of orders to the effect that he owed Ms Pinney over $100,000. By that time, Ms Pinney had incurred the costs in respect of which she now seeks an order.
The application for an injunction was reasonable in circumstances where it was not clear from Mr Pinney’s disclosure (or, rather, lack thereof) or evidence whether he did in fact have funds to comply with the orders. If I had been satisfied that he had the funds but was refusing to pay, I would likely have made the order sought. My decision was a finely balanced one.
The application for an order in relation to extra-curricular fees was for about $9,000. That aspect was a small part of the application for orders to the effect that more than $100,000 was owed. In those circumstances, I attach no weight to the fact that Ms Pinney was unsuccessful in obtaining an order for the full amount.

Conclusion

Having regard to the matters set out above and weighing, in particular, the fact that these proceedings were necessitated by serial and ongoing failures by Mr Pinney to comply with orders of the Court, I am satisfied it is just that Mr Pinney be ordered to pay Ms Pinney’s costs of and associated with the enforcement proceedings. There having been no evidence that Mr Pinney attempted to settle the costs application, I am also satisfied it is just that he pays her costs associated with that application.

ON WHAT BASIS SHOULD THE COSTS BE PAID?

Given I am satisfied that an order for costs is just, I then need to determine on what basis such costs should be paid. The matters set out above are relevant to that decision, as are the matters set out in rr 12.08 and 12.17 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules (Cth) (“the Rules”), and s 95 of the Act (“[o]verarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions”).
The different bases upon which costs can be ordered are set out in s 114UB(5), which provides:
(5) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceedings, or part of an amount assessed in accordance with the applicable Rules of Court.
The default position is that where the Court makes a costs order but does not specify the method for calculation of costs, they are to be assessed on a party and party basis (s 114UB(6)).
Ms Pinney seeks her costs of the enforcement proceedings on a party and party basis fixed in the sum of $34,831.85 or, in the alternative, on the scale at $24,665.59. She also seeks her costs of the costs application fixed in the sum of $5,000 or on the scale at $3,250.04.
I am satisfied, particularly given Ms Pinney was not successful in relation to the injunctive relief sought, that costs in the enforcement proceedings should be paid in accordance with the relevant scales and not more. The scale for Division 2 proceedings (which the enforcement proceedings were initially) is set out in sch 1 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth). The scale for Division 1 proceedings (which the enforcement proceedings became after they were transferred) is set out in sch 3 of the Rules.
In evidence is an unchallenged costs schedule (Annexure 2 to Ms Pinney’s affidavit), which supports the amounts sought. In respect of the costs application, it records costs on a party and party basis as $4,807.87, and on an indemnity basis as $6,868.39.
I am satisfied that, in relation to the enforcement application, the sum of $24,665.59 reflects the relevant scales. Making an order in a fixed sum is consistent with the overarching purpose of the practice and procedure provisions which is contained in s 95 of the Act; it promotes a just, inexpensive, timely and efficient resolution of the issue, rather than extending the resolution by the need for an assessment. This is particularly important given the tortuous history of this matter (where litigation commenced in 2022 and has now involved two final hearings and two sets of enforcement proceedings). I am satisfied that the sum of $24,665.59 is a fair, reasonable and proportionate sum in respect of the costs of the enforcement proceedings.
I am satisfied that the sum of $5,000 is a fair, reasonable and proportionate sum for a costs application which required the preparation of affidavit material and written submissions. It is more than costs on the scale but less than an indemnity. I therefore order that Mr Pinney pay Ms Pinney’s costs of the costs application fixed in that sum.
The orders will provide for Mr Pinney to pay to Ms Pinney the total sum of $29,665.59 within 28 days.
